mnemonics.title: AND Gate Remember
mnemonics.content: FOR AND GATE - ALL MUST BE 1: Remember: A-1, B-1, Output-1. Think: A AND B both need to be TRUE for TRUE output.

mnemonics.title: OR Gate Remember
mnemonics.content: FOR OR GATE - ANY ONE CAN BE 1: Remember: If A=1 OR B=1 (or both), Output=1. Think: Just need ONE true input.

mnemonics.title: NOT Gate Remember
mnemonics.content: NOT GATE - SWAPS IT: 0 becomes 1, 1 becomes 0. Think: It always gives the OPPOSITE.

mnemonics.title: Combinational vs Sequential
mnemonics.content: COMBINATIONAL = CURRENT (no memory) | SEQUENTIAL = PAST + PRESENT (has memory). Think: Sequential stores history.

acronyms.usage: A family of digital circuits using transistors, operating at +5V supply with 0V as logic 0 and +5V as logic 1
A technology for constructing integrated circuits, known for low power consumption
The field of engineering dealing with design and analysis of digital circuits using binary digits
The process of creating integrated circuits by combining thousands of transistors into a single chip

visualTips.visualization: Think of a simple light switch: OFF = 0, ON = 1. There is no in-between, just like a digital signal.
Draw gates as standard symbols: AND is like a D-shape (all must complete), OR is curved like a shield (one or more), NOT is a triangle with circle (inverts).
Organize inputs in binary counting order (00, 01, 10, 11) to ensure all combinations are covered systematically.
Think of a dimmer switch (analog - continuous) vs a light switch (digital - only two positions). Digital is simpler with clear boundaries.

analogies.analogy: Like a series circuit with two switches: both must be ON for current to flow and light to turn on.
Like parallel switches: if either switch is ON, current flows and light turns on.
Like a Normally Open relay: when input is ON (1), it opens and output is OFF (0), and vice versa.
Combinational is like a calculator: same inputs always give same outputs. Sequential is like reading a book: output depends on what page you're on (past) plus what you read now.
Like asking someone to nod (1) or shake head (0) - clear, unambiguous answers. Analog would be asking 'how much do you agree?' with any response from 0 to 100.
